name: Dynamic Pricing
description: A pricing rule set that changes item costs or sell values based on context, demand, reputation, scarcity, or time.
design_notes: Dynamic pricing creates economic texture only when players can understand the cause of a price change. Hidden formulas can feel arbitrary, so each modifier should have a visible reason, a stable boundary, and protection against exploits such as buy-sell loops.
design_purpose: Make shops respond to scarcity, faction status, demand, or player behavior.
Create timing and routing decisions around when and where to buy or sell.
Support economy sinks without relying on static prices for every item and game phase.
player_fantasy: The player feels like they are reading a market and choosing the right moment to trade.

parameters.name: price_curve_id
parameters.description: Identifier for the formula or lookup table used to calculate final price.

parameters.name: base_price_minor_units
parameters.description: Unmodified price stored in minor currency units before modifiers are applied.

parameters.name: stock_pressure_multiplier
parameters.description: Modifier derived from vendor stock, regional scarcity, or supply/demand state.

parameters.name: sellback_ratio
parameters.description: Fraction of original buy price returned when the player sells an item back.

parameters.name: price_floor_minor_units
parameters.description: Minimum allowed final price to prevent zero-cost purchases or infinite arbitrage.

edge_cases: A price changes between confirmation and commit, so the transaction must decide whether to honor the quoted price or reject it.
Discounts and sellback ratios can combine into a profitable buy-sell loop if final prices are not checked together.
Vendor stock refreshes while the player has a purchase dialog open.
A balance patch changes price_curve_id values for items already listed in player-owned shops or orders.
common_bugs: UI displays rounded prices while the transaction debits unrounded minor units, creating visible balance mismatches.
Client-side discount calculations are trusted by the server and allow edited price requests.
Price caches do not invalidate after reputation or stock changes, so players see stale prices.
Negative modifiers are applied after floor clamps and produce free or negative-cost purchases.
balancing_notes: Keep the visible reason for a price change close to the price display so players can plan rather than guess.
Simulate repeated buying and selling with all modifiers active; arbitrage bugs often appear only after several systems combine.
Use price floors, sellback caps, and purchase limits to protect against inflation without making every vendor feel identical.
accessibility_notes: Show final price, previous price, and reason for change in text as well as color.
Avoid time-limited price changes that require rushed decisions unless the player can review the item details comfortably.

implementation_notes.unity: Keep price calculation in a pure pricing service that takes item_id, vendor_id, price_curve_id, stock state, and reputation; purchase UI should display a quote_id that the commit step validates.
implementation_notes.godot: Store price curves as Resources or JSON rows and require buy/sell calls to pass a quoted price plus vendor stock version before mutating the wallet.
implementation_notes.unreal: Run pricing on the authoritative vendor or economy subsystem; replicated UI can show quotes, but server purchase validation must recompute or verify price_curve_id and quote freshness.
implementation_notes.web: Use integer minor-unit math and keep a transaction log with quoted_price, final_price, modifiers, and transaction_id so pricing bugs are debuggable after a session.

relationships.reason: Dynamic pricing usually applies at vendor offers where quotes, stock, and wallet transactions meet.
Market-like price curves can inform player trade expectations and prevent obvious arbitrage loops.
Faction reputation can provide a readable reason for discounts, markups, or restricted pricing.

example_games.title: Animal Crossing
example_games.note: Uses changing item values and sell opportunities as part of its economy loop.

example_games.title: Mount & Blade
example_games.note: Uses town economies and trade prices as strategic information.
